questions.prompt: Choose the MOST formal equivalent of the phrase "get rid of" in an official report.

options.text: throw away
do away with
eliminate
chuck out

questions.explanation: "Eliminate" is the Latinate single-word verb appropriate for an official report. "Do away with" is a slightly formal phrasal verb but still a multi-word construction. "Throw away" is an informal phrasal verb, and "chuck out" is highly colloquial. In formal academic or official writing, a single Latinate verb always outranks a multi-word equivalent.

options.text: They decided to restructure the department.
The restructuring of the department was authorised.
They will restructure the department soon.
Restructuring is what they are going to do.

questions.explanation: In the second sentence, the verb "restructure" has been converted into the noun phrase "the restructuring" — this is nominalisation. The sentence also uses an impersonal passive (was authorised), compounding the formal effect. The other options use restructure as a verb or gerund in a more personal, active construction — not full nominalisation.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is written in the MOST formal register?

options.text: It's crucial that we look into this ASAP.
The matter requires urgent investigation.
We really need to check this out quickly.
Someone should look at this pretty fast.

questions.explanation: The second option uses nominalisation (investigation), Latinate vocabulary (requires, urgent), and avoids contractions, phrasal verbs, and filler adverbs — all hallmarks of formal register. The first uses a contraction (it's) and an abbreviation (ASAP). The third uses emphatic adverbs and a phrasal verb (check out). The fourth is vague and informal (someone, pretty fast).

questions.prompt: Find the sentence with a REGISTER CLASH — an inappropriate mix of formal and informal.

options.text: We regret to inform you that your application was unsuccessful.
The committee resolved to defer the matter pending further analysis.
An investigation has been launched into the alleged irregularities.
The tribunal's findings are pretty damning for the management team.

questions.explanation: "Pretty damning" uses an informal intensifier (pretty) in what is otherwise a formal legal context (tribunal's findings). A consistent formal register would say "deeply damaging" or "severely critical". The other three sentences maintain consistent formal register throughout — no contractions, no informal adverbs, no colloquial vocabulary.

questions.prompt:
Transform this sentence into fully formal academic writing: "Researchers found that the drug makes pain go down a lot."
Choose the BEST version.

options.text: Researchers found that the drug makes pain decrease considerably.
Research findings indicate a significant reduction in pain associated with the drug.
The drug was found to reduce pain by a lot, according to researchers.
It was found by researchers that pain goes down a lot from the drug.

questions.explanation: The second option achieves the most complete transformation: nominalisation (findings, reduction), Latinate vocabulary (indicate, associated), impersonal passive construction (is implied through the noun phrase structure), and no informal quantifier (a lot → significant). The first replaces only the phrasal verb and a lot. The third keeps "a lot". The fourth uses the passive but retains "goes down a lot" and "a lot".

questions.prompt:
Read this extract from a formal letter: "We write to acknowledge receipt of your complaint dated 12 May and to inform you that a full ___ has been initiated."
Which nominalisation BEST completes the sentence?

options.text: look-into
checking-out
investigation
find-out

questions.explanation: "Investigation" is the Latinate nominalisation of "investigate" — the standard formal noun for an official enquiry. "Look-into" and "checking-out" are informal phrasal verb nominalisations (hyphenated) that belong to colloquial register and never appear in formal correspondence. "Find-out" is not a standard nominalisation at all — it is simply a hyphenated informal expression.
